The rep committed 19 May to send ROI-building questions to help Anand quantify the value case for the 23 Jun board meeting, but there is no confirmation those questions were answered or submitted. Without a quantified business case, the board approval (the critical gate that just passed) likely lacked the financial grounding to secure budget. The gap is a knowledge gap: the business problem is understood qualitatively, but the financial impact remains unknown and uncommitted.

competition.narrative: Esker is the incumbent and the primary competitor. On 7 May Anand stated that Esker is currently used for ERP (Next Step 7 May), meaning Corus already has Esker deployed and Zycus is positioning to replace or augment it. The competitive read is defensive displacement: Corus is not running a bake-off or formal RFP (no other vendors named), but the board must approve budget to switch or expand beyond Esker. The wedge is AI and end-to-end P2P scope: the 19 May demo emphasized conversational intake (Merlin), automated risk assessments (iRisk), and supplier onboarding (iSource, iCompliance) across the full S2P suite (Next Step 19 May), capabilities Esker (primarily an AP/invoice automation platform) does not natively offer at the same breadth. However, the buyer has not explicitly said Esker is failing or insufficient, so the competitive advantage is unconfirmed. The risk: if the board meeting on 23 Jun resulted in a decision to stay with Esker or defer investment, we have no visibility into the rationale or how to reopen the conversation. The activation for the next conversation is to make the Merlin agentic intake and iRisk automated compliance the centerpiece of the ROI reframe, contrasting Zycus's AI-native P2P with Esker's AP-only footprint.

Zycus is positioning to displace or expand beyond Esker with AI-native P2P capabilities (Merlin conversational intake, iRisk automated risk assessments, full S2P suite). No formal RFP or bake-off; the competition is budget approval to invest in Zycus versus staying with Esker or deferring. The wedge is AI breadth (agentic intake, compliance automation) versus Esker's AP-only footprint, but the buyer has not confirmed Esker is insufficient or failing. With the board meeting outcome unknown, competitive position is uncertain.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong on paper but unconfirmed in execution. The product scope includes Merlin, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI, iRisk, and iCompliance. The 19 May demo emphasized conversational intake, automated risk assessments, and S2P suite overview (Next Step 19 May), all AI-native capabilities that differentiate Zycus from Esker's AP-only footprint. However, there is no AIS score, no buyer quote confirming AI appetite, and no evidence the AI narrative landed with the board. The board meeting on 23 Jun was the moment to make AI the wedge, but with no outcome confirmation we do not know if the AI story resonated or if the buyer prioritized cost/risk over innovation. AI positioning is present but unvalidated. Not under-positioned, but effectiveness is unknown.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Buyer expectations are AI-native P2P transformation, security/compliance rigor, and ROI justification for board approval. Zycus is partially aligned: the 19 May demo covered conversational intake, automated risk assessments, and S2P suite overview, and the product scope includes Merlin, iRisk, iCompliance. However, ROI questions promised 19 May were not confirmed answered, and security customer references (Federal, BFSI, healthcare) were not confirmed delivered. Without these, the board presentation likely lacked the financial and trust anchors needed for approval. The fit is strong on capability, weak on execution and proof.
